text: The average length of a U.S. presidential speech fell from over five thousand words in the nineteenth century to under two thousand by the twentieth, and continues to compress. Historians of rhetoric note a parallel narrowing in argumentative complexity: Lincoln's Cooper Union address spent hours building a single sustained legal and historical case; contemporary political communication operates in assertions, not arguments. This is not a story of declining intelligence but of incentive structures — when audiences have been trained by short-form media to expect conclusions without premises, speakers who provide premises are punished with inattention.
question: What does the passage say happened to the average length of U.S. presidential speeches over time?

options.text: It remained stable but became more technically complex
It increased as education levels rose in the twentieth century
It fell from over five thousand words in the nineteenth century to under two thousand by the twentieth
It varied unpredictably depending on the political party in power
